g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Race Conditions, a critical topic in concurrent programming.
A race condition occurs when multiple processes access shared data concurrently, and the final outcome depends on timing.
Race conditions are dangerous because they may not appear during testing but can cause serious issues in production systems.
For a race condition to occur, three conditions must be satisfied: concurrency, shared resources, and modification.
The critical section is the code region accessing shared resources that must not be executed by more than one process at a time.
g.text: Only ONE process at a time!

g.data-narration: There are three main types: Read-Modify-Write, Check-Then-Act, and Write-Write races, each with different access patterns.
g.text: Check condition Act based on it

g.data-narration: In this bank example, both processes read the same balance before either writes, causing a lost update of $500.
g.text: Lost Update: $500 was lost! Both read 1000 before either wrote.

g.data-narration: In ticket booking, both users read available=1 before either decrements, leading to double booking of the same seat.
g.text: Read: 1 Check greater than 0 Decrement: 0 Confirm Booking
Read: 1 Check greater than 0 Decrement: 0 Confirm Booking
PROBLEM: Both users get confirmed for the SAME ticket!

g.data-narration: The counter increment example shows assembly-level details of how race conditions cause lost updates at the CPU level.
g.text: MOV eax, [counter] Load 0 (P1 has not stored yet)

g.data-narration: Race conditions in operating systems occur in process scheduling, file access, memory management, and device management.
g.text: Race conditions in kernel code can lead to system crashes!

g.data-narration: Solutions include atomic operations, mutexes, semaphores, and monitors to ensure mutual exclusion in critical sections.
g.text: These ensure only ONE process can enter the critical section at a time!

g.data-narration: Key points: Race conditions depend on timing, occur in critical sections, and are prevented by making operations atomic.
Race conditions cause incorrect results due to timing, while deadlocks cause processes to wait indefinitely for resources.
g.text: Different concurrency problems - do not confuse them! Race = wrong output, Deadlock = no progress

g.data-narration: For exams: Define clearly, remember three conditions, connect to critical sections, and give real-world examples.
